title: Revealed Mistakes and Revealed Preferences
abstract: Because some are not able to assess all the possible options for making a decision of an action, people often defy one of the most fundamental and traditional concepts in economics by going for an option that they would not have chosen had they been able to push through with thorough evaluation. Because of this, it would be useful to first analyze the common mistakes made until they become uncommon and until we are able to integrate what we have learned from these mistakes into creating models, policy prescriptions, and evaluations. Although certain fears may arise from such a measure on both the positive and normative side through mistakes becoming a free parameter and because of how the revealed-preference approach cannot be used, this chapter suggests other measures to deal with such concerns.
summary_en: When people make systematic mistakes that they later regret, how should economists incorporate this into welfare analysis? Köszegi and Rabin argue for studying common decision mistakes — and their corrections as people become better-informed — as a foundation for behavioural-economics modelling, policy prescription, and evaluation. They acknowledge methodological concerns: making mistakes a free parameter could undermine the discipline of revealed-preference welfare analysis. The chapter proposes ways to address these concerns while still using observed mistakes as informative welfare data. A foundational contribution to the methodology of behavioural welfare economics.
data_used: Theoretical / methodological chapter. No empirical contribution; argues for a research-programme reorientation in behavioural welfare economics.
policy_relevance: Foundational for Hungarian consumer-protection and behavioural-policy-design discussions (MNB, GVH, NFH). The Köszegi-Rabin framework is the methodological backbone for distinguishing 'paternalism is unjustified because people freely chose' from 'paternalism is justified because choices reflect identifiable mistakes'. Useful reference for any Hungarian behavioural-policy intervention (default-savings, financial education, addiction-product regulation) that needs welfare justification.
